supporting_evidence: Transient visual disturbance and confusion lasting minutes, now fully resolved
Age 72, male, with poorly controlled hypertension and type 2 diabetes — classic TIA risk profile
ABCD2 score likely 4-5 (age >60, BP >140/90, duration <60 min, diabetes)
No residual focal neurological deficit on presentation
Dark/black stools (melena) — classic presentation of upper GI haemorrhage
Aspirin 75 mg daily — direct risk factor for gastric erosion and peptic ulcer disease
HR 98 may reflect mild hypovolaemia from ongoing blood loss
Age >65 increases risk of aspirin-related GI complications
Known bicuspid aortic valve and dilated aortic root (4.2 cm)
However, no classic tearing chest or back pain reported
Two separate symptom complexes could theoretically represent malperfusion — but this is a less common presentation
Age 72 with hypertension and diabetes — high CHA2DS2-VASc score
Transient neurological symptoms could represent cardioembolic TIA
No known history of AF, but paroxysmal AF may be undiagnosed
Chronic aspirin use is a major risk factor for peptic ulceration
Melena consistent with upper GI source
No reported epigastric pain, but elderly patients may have painless presentation

reasoning: 72-year-old male with two distinct symptom complexes: transient neurological deficit (resolved) and melena. The differential is split between vascular/neurological causes (TIA, cardioembolic event) and GI causes (upper GI bleeding from aspirin use). Aortic dissection is flagged as a don't-miss diagnosis given his known bicuspid valve and dilated aortic root, though the absence of chest pain makes it a lower-probability consideration. The two complaints are most likely separate pathologies — TIA in a high-risk vascular patient plus aspirin-related GI bleeding — but aortic malperfusion as a unifying diagnosis cannot be excluded without imaging.
